pages.text: AI Convergence [ Bigdata Analytics ] linear Regression DaeHan Ahn

Regression Analysis ΎΒυ What is Regression ? ΎΓπ Regression analysis is a statistical method used to model the relationship between  independent variables (features) and a  dependent variable (target) in order to  predict continuous numerical values. ΎΒυ Purpose of Regression ΎΓπ Understand how input variables affect the target variable ΎΓπ Estimate unknown continuous values ΎΓπ Quantify the strength and direction of relationships ΎΓπ Support decision-making based on data ΎΒυ Types ΎΓπ Simple Regression : an independent variable  έΗΤ ΊΣοΊΣο = ΊΣΛΊΣΛΊΣΛΊΣΛ + ΊΣΠΊΣΠ ΎΓπ Multiple Regression : more than 2 independent variables  έΗΤ ΊΣοΊΣο = ΊΣΛΊΣΛ 1 ΊΣΛΊΣΛ 1 + ΊΣΛΊΣΛ 2 ΊΣΛΊΣΛ 2 + έΜψ + ΊΣΠΊΣΠ ΎΓπ Logistic Regression : When the dependent variable is categorical (0 or 1)

Use It When... ΎΒυ 1. The target variable is continuous ΎΓπ How much will next year's revenue be? ΎΓπ What will this patient's blood pressure level be? ΎΒυ 2. You want to predict numerical values ΎΓπ If we increase ad spend by $10K, how much will sales grow? ΎΓπ If a student studies 1 more hour, how many more points will they score? ΎΒυ 3. You Want to Identify the Most Influential Variables ΎΓπ What drives house prices more  έΑΦ size or location? ΎΒυ 4. Your outcome is a continuous number -> Standard Regression  ΎΒυ 5. Your outcome is a category (Y/N, P/F) -> Logistic Regression

Limitations of Regression Analysis ΎΒυ 1. Assumes a Linear Relationship ΎΓπ If the real relationship is curved or complex, the model will be inaccurate ΎΒυ 2. Sensitive to Outliers ΎΓπ A few extreme data points can heavily distort the entire model ΎΓπ One bad data point can pull the regression line significantly off course ΎΒυ 3. Correlation έΚι Causation ΎΓπ Just because two variables are related doesn't mean one causes the other ΎΓπ e.g., Ice cream sales and drowning rates are correlated  έΑΦ but ice cream doesn't  cause drowning (both increase in summer) ΎΒυ 4. Multicollinearity (Multiple Regression) ΎΓπ When independent variables are too correlated with each other, coefficients  become unreliable ΎΒυ 5. Requires Clean, Sufficient Data ΎΓπ Missing values, small sample sizes, or biased data lead to poor results ΎΓπ Garbage in έΗΤ Garbage out

Basic Assumptions of Linear Regression ΎΒυ 1. Linearity ΎΓπ The relationship between independent variables and the dependent variable should be  approximately linear ΎΓπ Check with: Scatter plot, Residual vs Fitted plot ΎΓπ Solution: apply log/polynomial transformation ΎΒυ 2. Homoscedasticity (Constant Variance) ΎΓπ The variance of residuals (errors) should be consistent across all levels of X ΎΓπ Check with: Residual plot, Scatter plot ΎΓπ Solution:  use weighted regression  ΎΒυ 3. Normality of Residuals ΎΓπ The residuals (errors) should be normally distributed ΎΓπ Check with:  Histogram, Q -Q plot, Shapiro -Wilk test ΎΓπ Solution:  increase sample size  ΎΒυ 4. No Multicollinearity (Multiple Regression only) ΎΓπ Independent variables should not be highly correlated with each other ΎΓπ High correlation makes coefficients unstable and uninterpretable ΎΓπ Check with: VIF (Variance Inflation Factor) ΎΓπ Solution: remove or combine correlated variables

Evaluation Metrics for Linear Regression ΎΒυ 1. Mean Absolute Error (MAE) ΎΓπ Measures the average absolute difference between actual and predicted values ΎΓπ MAE =  1 ΊΣδΊΣδ έΙΣ ΊΣοΊΣο ΊΣΨΊΣΨ έΙΤ Ύ┐╜ ΊΣοΊΣο ΊΣΨΊΣΨ ΎΓπ Easy to interpret ΎΓπ Same unit as target variable ΎΒυ 2. Mean Squared Error (MSE) ΎΓπ Squares errors before averaging ΎΓπ MSE =  1 ΊΣδΊΣδ έΙΣ ΊΣοΊΣο ΊΣΨΊΣΨ έΙΤ Ύ┐╜ ΊΣοΊΣο ΊΣΨΊΣΨ 2 ΎΓπ Penalizes large errors strongly ΎΓπ Sensitive to outliers ΎΒυ 3. Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) ΎΓπ Square root of MSE ΎΓπ RMSE =  = 1 ΊΣδΊΣδ έΙΣ ΊΣοΊΣο ΊΣΨΊΣΨ έΙΤ Ύ┐╜ ΊΣοΊΣο ΊΣΨΊΣΨ 2 ΎΓπ Same unit as target variable ΎΓπ Widely used in practice ΎΒυ 4. R -squared (Coefficient of Determination) ΎΓπ Measures how much variance is explained by the model ΎΓπ ΊΣΖΊΣΖ 2 = 1 έΙΤ έΙΣ ΊΣοΊΣο ΊΣΨΊΣΨ έΙΤ Ύ┐╜ ΊΣοΊΣο ΊΣΨΊΣΨ 2 έΙΣ ΊΣοΊΣο ΊΣΨΊΣΨ έΙΤ Ύ┐╜ ΊΣοΊΣο 2 ΎΓπ Value closer to 1 means better fit ΎΓπ Easy to compare models

Practice: Linear Regression (Scikit -learn) ΎΒυ Generate Dataset import numpy as  np import matplotlib .pyplot as  plt # Generate Dataset np .random .seed   (42 )  X = np .random .randint (0,  101  , size =(100  ,  1))  # Independent Variable (0~100 value, #100) y = 2 * X + np .random .randint (0,  51 , size =(100  ,  1))  #  Target variable (add noise) # Visualization plt  .scatter (X,  y)  plt  .title ('Dataset' ) plt  .xlabel ('X'  ) plt  .ylabel ('y'  ) plt  .show   ()

Practice: Linear Regression (Scikit -learn) ΎΒυ Train Regression Model ΎΓπ Q1: Import  LinearRegression  from  Sklearn  library ΎΓπ Q2: Train the model using έΑα fit έΑβ function from sklearn .linear_model  import ___________ model =  ______________() model.___(X, y)

Practice: Linear Regression (Scikit -learn) ΎΒυ Train Regression Model ΎΓπ Q: Predict Y for input X, and plot the regression line using plt.plot  (matplotlib) y_pred  = model._____(X) plt.scatter( X, y )  plt.____(X,  y_pred , color ='red') plt.title('Linear Regression') plt.xlabel('X') plt.ylabel('y') plt.show()

Practice: Linear Regression ( Statsmodels ) ΎΒυ Statsmodels ΎΓπ It basically doesnέΑβt have an intercept (bias b) ΎΓπ Q:  Add the intercept using έΑα add_constant έΑβ import  numpy  as  np import  statsmodels .api as  sm import  matplotlib. pyplot  as plt # Generate Dataset np.random.seed(42 )  X = np. random.randint(0,  101, size =( 100, 1))  # Independent Variable (0~100 value, #100) y = 2 *  X +  np.random.randint( 0, 51 , size=( 100,  1))  # Target variable (add noise) # add intercept (bias b) X_sm =  sm._______( X) # OLS (Ordinary Least Squares) model and train model =  sm.OLS(y , X_sm) results  = model. ___() print(results.summary())
Practice: Linear Regression ( Statsmodels ) ΎΒυ Statsmodels  (Result)

Practice: Linear Regression ( Statsmodels ) ΎΒυ Linearity pred =  results.predict(X_sm) plt.scatter( pred , y) plt.xlabel("Prediction") plt.ylabel("Target Values") plt.title("Prediction vs. Target Values") plt.show() If the data points generally form a straight - line pattern, the relationship can be considered linear If a curved shape or complex pattern  appears, it may indicate a nonlinear  relationship

Practice: Linear Regression ( Statsmodels ) ΎΒυ Normality of Residuals ΎΓπ Shapiro- Wilk test έΑλ It takes a value between 0 and 1 έΑλ A value closer to 1 indicates that the distribution is close to a normal  distribution from scipy import  stats # Evaluate residual residuals =  results.resid # Shapiro-Wilk test shapiro_test  = stats.shapiro(residuals) print("Shapiro-Wilk Test: \nStatistic :", shapiro_test [0 ], "\np -Value:", shapiro_test[ 1])

Practice: Linear Regression ( Statsmodels ) ΎΒυ Normality of Residuals ΎΓπ Q-Q plot έΑλ A QQ plot (Quantile έΑΥQuantile Plot) is a graph that compares the  quantiles of sample data with the quantiles of a normal distribution έΑλ If the data follows a normal distribution, the points are generally  drawn close to a straight line # Q-Q Plot plt.figure(figsize =(6, 4)) sm.qqplot(residuals, line ='s') plt.title('Q -Q Plot') plt.show()

Practice: Linear Regression ( Statsmodels ) ΎΒυ Homoscedasticity ΎΓπ Breusch έΑΥPagan Test έΑλ The  BreuschέΑΥPagan test  is a statistical method used to examine whether the  assumption of  homoscedasticity  is valid in a linear regression model έΑλ The  p-value is more important than the test statistic itself έΑΧ p- value έΚν 0.05:  The test is statistically significant, indicating that heteroscedasticity  may exist in the residuals έΑΧ p- value > 0.05:  The test is not statistically significant, suggesting that assuming  homoscedasticity of residuals is reasonable from    statsmodels .stats .diagnostic  import  het_breuschpagan # Evaluate Residuals residuals  = results .resid # Breusch -Pagan test bp_test  = het_breuschpagan (residuals ,  results .model.exog ) print ("Breusch -Pagan Test: \nStatistic :" ,  bp_test [0],   "\np-Value:" ,  bp_test [1])

Practice: Linear Regression ( Statsmodels ) ΎΒυ Multicollinearity import  pandas  as  pd np .random .seed   (42 ) X1  = np .random .randint (0,  101  ,  100  )  X2  = X1  + np .random .normal (0,  3,  100  )  X3  = np .random .randint (50 ,  201  ,  100  )  y = 2 * X1  + 4 * X2  + 1 * X3  + np .random .normal (0,  5,  100  )  df  = pd .DataFrame ({ 'X1'   :  X1 ,  'X2'   :  X2 ,  'X3'   :  X3 ,  'y'  :  y}) df .head   () from    statsmodels .stats .outliers_influence  import  variance_inflation_factor # Evaluate VIF X = df [[ 'X1'   ,  'X2'   ,  'X3'   ]] X = sm .add_constant (X) vif_data  = pd .DataFrame () vif_data ["feature" ]  = X.columns vif_data ["VIF" ]  = [variance_inflation_factor (X.values ,  i)  for   i in  range (X.shape [1])] print (vif_data ) VIF close to 1 generally indicates little or no multicollinearity έΑλ 1<VIF<5: Acceptable level έΑλ 5<VIF<10: Moderate multicollinearity έΑλ VIF>10: variable removal or transformation should be considered
